Twitter has filed a lawsuit in the Delaware Court of Chancery to hold Elon Musk accountable to his contractual obligations.
Here we go: Twitter sues Elon Musk https://twitter.com/...
obviously the poop emoji made it into the complaint https://twitter.com/...
Twitter's lawyers: This man is a complete joke! Also Twitter's lawyers: And that's why he should own Twitter.

Big ouch for A16Z, Sequoia, Ellison, etc: Musk's investors, all sophisticated market participants, made these commitments in the face of Musk's public statements regarding spam accounts, and knowing he had forsworn diligence.
What is Musk's team doing with the firehose data? Seems out of scope for the mdau / spam issue https://twitter.com/...

Twitter's lead lawyer is Bill Savitt, co-chair of litigation at Wachtell Lipton and a top Delaware Chancery litigator. Brad Sorrels of Wilson Sonsini, another Delaware Chancery veteran, is also on the complaint. Local counsel is Potter Anderson & Corroon. https://twitter.com/...
Twitter sues Elon Musk, calling his attempts to weasel out of the deal “a model of hypocrisy” and “a model of bad faith.” https://t.co/iWpLK9h7Wf https://t.co/DJ0xQjQYyC
full internal memo from Twitter ceo Parag Agrawal to employees this afternoon: (in response to an earlier widely sent email from general counsel Sean Edgett) https://twitter.com/...

The Twitter suit is blistering and just ... obviously true? You can't buy a distressed property and brag to neighbors about how you're going to fix the horrible termite problem ... and then cry after signing bc you're astonished to find termites in the walk-through. https://twitter.com/...

I do still think it would be surprising if the court orders Musk to fully buy Twitter, but it's not impossible at this point and honestly Musk might be lucky if he can get out of this for just a few billion.

Why @Twitter? Did you hear the Twitter employee witness at the #January6thHearings today describing how your platform was used to foment a violent mob attack on our govt by the man Musk wants to invite back on? What are your full fiduciary responsibilities? https://twitter.com/...
Musk's data team got access to the Twitter firehouse and ran 100,000 queries in two weeks. Seems like a lot. https://twitter.com/...

As it promised, Twitter has sued Elon Musk for backing out of the deal in its incorporated state of Delaware. Spicy line from the filing: “Having mounted a public spectacle to put Twitter in play, and having proposed and then signed a seller-friendly merger agreement...
“Rather than bear the cost of the market downturn.. Musk wants to shift it to Twitter's stockholders” https://t.co/cCwQoR24rh

Quick takeaways from @twitter lawsuit: -Alleges Musk strategy “a model of bad faith” & “model of hypocrisy” -Alleges he “violated his obligations to treat requests for consent reasonably” -Says his disparaging public comments created “business risk” & “downward pressure” on stock https://twitter.com/...
text of internal memo from Twitter's general counsel to employees https://t.co/kFxWLgtXw5

Musk agreed to buy Twitter, but “apparently believes that he — unlike every other party subject to Delaware contract law — is free to change his mind, trash the company, disrupt its operations, destroy stockholder value, and walk away,” Twitter's suit says https://www.documentcloud.org/ ...
this is no dry legal brief almost as if it was meant to be tweeted and as a friend noted, aimed at winning both in the Court of Public Opinion as well as Delaware Chancery https://twitter.com/...
As expected, Twitter is asking for “specific performance” — for Elon Musk to go through with his contractual agreement to buy Twitter. https://t.co/b8yA9grEkn

Suit on bots: Elon's “information requests were designed to try to tank the deal. Musk's increasingly outlandish requests reflect not a genuine examination of Twitter's processes but a litigation-driven campaign to try to create a record of non-cooperation on Twitter's part.”

TWITTER not mincing words: “Musk apparently believes that he— unlike every other party subject to Delaware contract law — is free to change his mind, trash the company, disrupt its operations, destroy stockholder value, and walk away.”
Imagine the good that could otherwise be done with all the legal fees for this mess https://twitter.com/...

“For Musk, it would seem, Twitter, the interests of its stockholders, the transaction Musk agreed to, and the court process to enforce it all constitute an elaborate joke.” https://www.washingtonpost.com/ ... https://twitter.com/...
We will force this man unfit to run the company to buy the company for the good of the company https://twitter.com/...

$TWTR just did Elon Musk a huge favor by filing today. Normally, Musk runs damage control by scheduling events ("launches" of products but perhaps also rockets) when bad news is scheduled to run. Today, Wachtell and WSGR (which also works for $TSLA) gave him cover for free.

I'll never forget the strange Muskrats screaming about how this was gonna be some kind of reckoning and all that's happened is Elon Musk losing money and continuing to lose money. https://twitter.com/...

They're not wrong here, but if they're doing this out of fiduciary duty, then fiduciary duty is deeply flawed. There's no way any of this anything but incredibly stupid. Make this all go away as soon as possible so staff and the company can have some certainty. https://t.co/C8SUyfph0Z
That's a strong complaint. They seek a sole remedy - specific performance. Obviously other remedies could emerge but for now, they seek one. https://twitter.com/...

@Kantrowitz Please then explain Twitter's rationale in filing this case. Wouldn't it make more sense to enforce billion dollar penalty, move on & hopefully stabilize the stock price? This case will make stock more volatile and cause them to lose even more. (And yes, aware of purchase price)
Wow, just look how hard Musk has worked to understand Twitter's process for measuring spam https://twitter.com/...

Twitter calling him “a model of hypocrisy” in a lawsuit to get him to buy the company... is just all-around hilarious and keeping in complete character with Twitter, actually https://www.theverge.com/...

Interesting nugget from the Twitter lawsuit: Twitter accuses Musk of “delaying and stymying key operational decisions”, including by not giving the go ahead for the company to launch 2 employee retention programs (Per the agreement, Musk has to sign off on decisions like this) https://twitter.com/...

Reminder: The Twitter complaint is just Twitter's version of events. The whole point is to make Twitter look good and Elon look not good. Obviously Elon will have a counter worthy of his super genius. If you thought his tweets were impressive... just wait! https://twitter.com/...
“Twitter also negotiated for itself a right to hire and fire employees at all levels, including executives, without having to seek Musk's consent.”

Hmm! When Twitter gave Elon Musk its firehouse data, he and his team ran *search* queries, like any user would search terms, it sounds like. He pretended this was about bots. But what if he was searching for something else in the firehose data? https://twitter.com/...

Sounds like Twitter's legal team is hoping to get this to trial as early as mid-September and believes the trial can be done in just 4 days (!), according to a source. Ambitious! regardless, they clearly want to move fast. https://twitter.com/...
In this lawsuit, Twitter finds a lot of ways to call Musk a liar. Among vocabulary used: “pretending,” “conjure,” “asserted, falsely,” “bad faith.”

What's funny about Twitter's lawsuit against El*n is that they're NOT looking for the $1 bn breakup fee. Weirdly, Twitter's argument is that Elon has done so much damage to the company that he should acquire it. Essentially, “you break it, you buy it.” https://www.documentcloud.org/ ... https://twitter.com/...
The 62-page complaint filed in the Delaware Chancery Court: https://t.co/VIcVdjaPmE https://t.co/8IUrNkPdTa

twitter ceo parag agrawal just sent out an internal memo basically aimed at calming the troops. “We took the time to defend our company, our people and our stockholders. We plan to hold the buyer fully accountable to fulfill his contractual obligations.”

The billionaire who proclaimed he only wanted to “help” Twitter and “free speech online” is doing neither. You almost get the sense this was about self-interest the whole time and it would be naive to take his claims at face value. Almost. https://www.nytimes.com/...

How Musk damaged Twitter and let it worse off — He has eroded trust in Twitter, walloped employee morale, spooked potential advertisers, emphasized its financial difficulties, and spread misinformation about how the company operates https://www.nytimes.com/...

Folks have spent months trying to parse Elon's half baked thoughts on online content policy; usually reading in whatever they hoped to be true. Meanwhile, Twitter is in court in India to try to uphold free expression online in the face of creeping authoritarianism. https://twitter.com/...
The best thing Twitter could do for employee morale is give a big “FU” to Musk and ban him from the platform. https://www.wired.com/...

Please forgive my momentary lapse into meanness: turns out another-rich-white-guy-with-a-savior- complex, not the freakin savior of media. How many times have we seen this story in last few decades? https://twitter.com/...

*take 2* Ev stepping down as CEO of MEDIUM New CEO @tonystubblebine worked at Odeo way back in the day before Odeo morphed into Twitter https://t.co/rdR6N8Ltaj

Love this news — @tonystubblebine is a great choice to lead @Medium. I'm hoping they double-down on what they've been great at (for all the pivots & changes, they have a huge audience that's not dependent on shitty hot takes or surveillance-based ads) and help the indie web. https://twitter.com/...
RIP the nonstop news cycle of Ev-led Medium pivots My favorite was the “claps” era https://t.co/QIhZGmqFGB

Sad about this but I am grateful to @ev for once again—after Blogger and Twitter—giving more people more ways to speak online. He is a quiet visionary and I look forward to what comes next. Evan Williams Is Stepping Down as C.E.O. of Medium https://www.nytimes.com/...

BMW is now charging $18 per month for heated seats in some countries

This is wild — BMW is now selling a monthly subscription service for heated seats in your car. • Monthly fee: $18 • Annual fee: $180 The car will come with all the necessary components, but payment is needed to remove a software block. Welcome to microtransaction hell.

BMW charging a monthly subscription fee for features like heated seats is what happens when recurring revenue envy breaks corporate brains. Stop encouraging this madness by paying for these features, people. https://www.theverge.com/...

BMW starting to charge $18/mo for heated seats around the world is just the beginning — every car company is thinking about recurring revenue this way. Here's Mercedes CEO Ola Kallenius on Decoder a couple weeks ago: https://www.theverge.com/... https://twitter.com/...

this is interesting. the tech to heat the seats is already in the car. And I assume they've factored that into the base price. So they're effectively charging you a monthly fee to enable technology you already own and paid for. https://www.thedrive.com/...
A seat heater blocked by software is broken, and the car owner should have the right to repair their seats. https://twitter.com/... https://twitter.com/...
My heavens. Imagine the price for functioning brakes! https://twitter.com/...
Paying separately to use the stuff already in your car. Nasty work. https://twitter.com/...

my least favorite dystopic future is the one in which no direct, embodied relationship to the world is possible, and instead every single aspect of relation is pulled apart (e.g. self-to-body, self-to-others, body-to-world) to be mediated by some opaque and proprietary technology https://twitter.com/...
Next up: a subscription service for how many times per month you can use your brakes. https://www.theverge.com/...
Subscription based models are bullshit. I refuse to participate. Do not make me pay a monthly subscription to experience the utility of a physical good I ALREADY PAID FOR. https://twitter.com/...
Just wait until Blizzard starts making cars too. You won't even be able to change the damn radio station with a legendary gem or whatever. https://twitter.com/...

It sucks that this is probably going to work. $18 is chump change to BMW owners. They can either pay money they won't notice missing, or, what? Void their warranty that's worth tens of thousands of bucks? Be like the poors and NOT buy a dick-measuring car? Nah, they'll pay https://twitter.com/...

I'm going to lose it. If you're the person that pays for this, stop. Seriously. Please, stop. They won't do this if no one pays for it. Quit ruining it for the rest of us and you'll get it for free too https://www.thedrive.com/...
For $18/month, BMW is now selling a subscription service for heated car seats. The Fed needs to raise rates by 1,000 basis points.

The year is 2042. You just bought your new car, along with 10 subscriptions. One of them keeping your airbags active. Another one for GPS (with NFT skins & lootboxes). Oh and of course the standard 30 bucks/month to keep the engine from blowing up in your face https://twitter.com/...
remember when you could download a crack on limewire to bypass adobe's subscription checks for the software you used to be able to just buy https://twitter.com/... https://twitter.com/...

When we use the terms “technofeudalism” we're really talking about a sort of tax levied by right of ownership that existed in feudalism levied through some sort of individual obligation, and this is like the purest rent-seeking I've ever seen https://www.theverge.com/...
If I'm paying 40k+ for a BMW, I would not be stoked about paying microtransactions on top of that. https://twitter.com/...

I'm sorry, y'all. I am not interested in the places new cars are going. I don't want to pay a subscription for heated seats. I don't want to be tracked. I want to be able to fix it myself. My 2005 911 is a perfectly safe, reliable and fun daily driver. https://www.theverge.com/...

Then of course this opens an arms race with modders and hackers who'll want to bypass the systems. Opening the door to the right to repair debate, and maybe even FTC scrutiny if BMW declares enabling something you already own a warranty violation?

“Carmakers have always charged customers more money for high-end features, of course, but the dynamic is very different when software, rather than hardware, is the limiting factor.” https://www.theverge.com/...
Tesla caused this 🫠 We'll be seeing a lot more of this to allow manufacturers to keep making money after a car is sold on... https://twitter.com/...
Jailbreaking cars is definitely going to be a thing. https://www.theverge.com/...
Unscientific poll. Would you ever buy a car that charged subscription fees or basic features like heated seats? https://twitter.com/...
Executives from Amazon, Microsoft, Apple, Meta, Google, and 500+ business, education, and nonprofit leaders urge US states to boost computer science education
Computer Science: Opportunity for Every Student
A who's who of CEOs is begging every school to teach computer science
Wow! The CEOs of the largest companies and nonprofits in the United States have joined forces to make computer science a basic part of the K-12 curriculum. See who's on board: https://ceosforcs.com/

When I was 13, computer science changed the course of my life. I was really lucky to have access to a computer that early on. I hope this initiative will give every student the same opportunity. https://twitter.com/...

Coding is one of the most valuable skills a person can learn. It can open new doors, jumpstart careers, and help big dreams seem like achievable goals. Everyone around the world should have an opportunity to learn how to code. https://www.ceosforcs.com/
This was the definition of “no brainer” to sign on to. #ceosforcs https://twitter.com/...

Our schools should equip students with the knowledge & skills needed to navigate the digital economy. I am proud to join @codeorg in their letter to Governors & Educators, which calls for access to computer science education for each & every K-12 student. https://www.ceosforcs.com/...

Computer science opened up a world of opportunity for me. Every student should have the chance to learn one of our generation's superpowers. Even if it's just to make video games :) You can help K-12 students across America have more oppty via @codeorg https://www.ceosforcs.com/

We developed a Pan-Canadian Computer Science Framework to guide policymakers, curriculum developers, and educators in implementing Computer Science education in 🇨🇦 classrooms. And now look at the great work https://code.org/ is doing in the States! https://twitter.com/...

Computer science is a fundamental part of education, and that's why I joined more than 500 other leaders to call for every student to have the opportunity to learn computer science as a basic part of education. Learn more: https://www.ceosforcs.com/

Every job in every sector of the economy requires digital skills, from engineering to the arts. Yet only 5% of US high school students study computer science. Every student deserves the opportunity to build skills to be competitive in tomorrow's economy. https://www.ceosforcs.com/

Computer science is essential for almost every career. Every student in every school should have the chance to learn these skills. I'm proud Microsoft's @satyanadella and @BradSmi are signatories, alongside 500 CEOs and leaders across the nation. https://www.ceosforcs.com/ @codeorg

Computer science education is essential in today's world. We believe every student in every school should have the chance to learn it, which is why we've joined 500 CEOs and leaders across the nation to support making CS a basic part of K-12 education. https://www.ceosforcs.com/ https://twitter.com/...

At @HPE, we don't believe there is a talent shortage; we believe there is an experience shortage. Today, I joined 500 business and nonprofit leaders to call for every student in every school to have the opportunity to learn computer science. https://www.ceosforcs.com/

Today I join 500 business and nonprofit leaders to call for every student in every school to have the opportunity to learn computer science as a basic part of education. I encourage other leaders to sign, to better prepare our children for the future. https://www.ceosforcs.com/.

When I was young, I didn't see myself as someone who would work in tech. But I took a CS class in college and it changed the course of my career. Every student should have the opportunity to explore how computer science could impact their lives! https://www.ceosforcs.com/

Computer science is essential for almost every career in today's world. I believe every student in every school should have the chance to learn it, which is why I added my name to this letter, alongside 500 other CEOs and leaders across the nation. https://www.ceosforcs.com/

It's critical to better prepare the next generation for their future jobs. Alongside @codeorg and more than 500 CEOs, I'm honored to advocate for all students to have the opportunity to learn computer science and pursue careers in tech and beyond. https://www.ceosforcs.com/

Attackers behind a large-scale adversary-in-the-middle (AiTM) phishing campaign used stolen credentials and session cookies to skip the authentication process and perform follow-on business email compromise (BEC) campaigns against other targets. Details: https://www.microsoft.com/...

Before you criticize the Phone (1) having ‘only’ two rear cameras, I have two words to say: iPhone 13. Hah, he just subtweeted OnePlus using pro photographers to do its demos. https://twitter.com/...

The Nothing (1) would actually be interesting if it came to U.S. carriers where the mid-tier competition is mostly limited to Apple iPhone SE, Google's Pixel a, and Samsung's Galaxy A, but it won't. Outside the U.S., it will face a LOT of stiff competition at that price.

So is this much ado about Nothing? Yes and no. The Nothing (1) does not — cannot — live up to the hype it's gotten. It is not a threat to BBK/Xiaomi/Samsung/Apple. However, it might be interesting enough to sell in moderate volumes and create future opportunities. https://twitter.com/...

The FTC plans to crack down on tech companies' illegal use and sharing of “location, health, or other sensitive data” and false claims about data anonymization
FTC puts data collectors and brokers on notice in light of abortion bans
FTC Promises Crackdown on Illegal Misuse of Health Information and Ostensibly De-Identified Data
FTC suddenly gets very stern about not-really-anonymized anonymized data
FTC official vows to ‘crack down’ on companies misusing consumer health data

In post about sensitive health and location data, @FTC warns claims about data being anonymous or anonymized are “often deceptive.” “Companies that make false claims about anonymization can expect to hear from the FTC.”  https://www.ftc.gov/... https://twitter.com/...

2. Businesses seeking to reassure users may claim that they anonymize or aggregate data. But research shows that “anonymized” data can often be re-identified, especially in the context of location data. Claims that data is “anonymized” are often deceptive and can violate the law.

3. Sensitive data is protected by several legal provisions that we enforce, including the FTC Act, Safeguards Rule, Health Breach Notification Rule, & Children's Online Privacy Protection Rule. We'll continue cracking down on firms that unlawfully collect, retain, or misuse data.

1. Firms' collection and misuse of Americans' location, health, and other sensitive data can expose individuals to significant harm. @FTC will continue to use its full set of legal tools and authorities to protect people's privacy. https://www.ftc.gov/...

Advertisers and data brokers too often claim data is “anonymized” to dismiss concerns about the risks of collecting, aggregating, analyzing, buying, sharing, selling, or using it. @FTC is right that “Claims that data is “anonymous” or “has been anonymized” are often deceptive.” https://twitter.com/...

While this is not entirely unexpected, it is bad news for marketers. Now it will fall to marketers to ensure that data they are using in their engagement solutions is “sufficiently” anonymized to meet a somewhat undefined FTC standard for anonymization. How do we manage at scale? https://twitter.com/...

Glad to see this @FTC July 11 press release on location, health, and other sensitive information — drawing attention to and committing to focusing more on the sharing and brokering of highly sensitive personal data. https://www.ftc.gov/...
